title: Fitting an outside tap
subtitle: Tee off the cold mains supply and run a 15 mm pipe through the wall
excerpt: How to fit a standard outside tap: isolating the cold mains supply, drilling a sleeved hole through the external wall, fitting a tee and isolating valve on the supply, running 15 mm copper to an outside tap plate with a built-in backflow preventer, and commissioning.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. The Plumbing Regulations (Water Supply) (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999.

glossaryTerms.definition: A non-return valve required by the Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999 on any outside tap connected to the potable mains supply. It prevents garden hose water from being siphoned back into the drinking water supply if main pressure drops.

A small inline isolating valve (quarter-turn) fitted on the branch pipe feeding an appliance or fitting. Required on every outside tap branch so the tap can be isolated and drained for winter without turning off the whole house supply.

content.text: An outside tap must include a
under the Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999; most outside tap kits include one built into the wall plate. Fit a dedicated
on the indoor branch so the tap can be isolated and drained each winter.
Step 1, isolate the cold mains supply

Turn off the cold mains stopcock (usually under the kitchen sink or where the rising main enters the house). Open the nearest cold tap to confirm the supply is off and to drain the pressure from the pipe you will be cutting. Do not proceed until the tap runs dry. Scan the wall above the intended drill position with a cable-and-pipe detector before drilling.
Step 2, plan the route and drill the wall

Choose a position for the tap as close as possible to the cold mains supply pipe inside, to minimise the pipe run and the length of uninsulated pipe exposed to frost. Drill a 28 mm hole through the wall on a slight downward slope toward the outside so any condensation drains out. Fit a plastic or copper pipe sleeve through the hole. Most kit wall plates have a built-in sleeve boss.
Step 3, fit the tee and service valve on the mains pipe

Cut into the cold mains supply pipe and fit a 15 mm compression or solder tee. On copper pipe, clean the cut ends with emery cloth, apply flux, and solder the tee using lead-free solder. Fit a 15 mm service valve on the branch outlet of the tee. The service valve allows the outside tap to be isolated for winter without affecting the house water supply.
Step 4, run the pipe to the wall plate

Run 15 mm copper from the service valve outlet to the indoor connection of the wall plate, through the wall sleeve. Insulate any pipe in an unheated space (garage, outside wall cavity) with pipe lagging. Fix the wall plate to the outside wall with screws and plugs, threading the supply pipe through the sleeve. Wrap the wall plate connection thread with PTFE tape before making the connection.

Restore the mains supply slowly with the service valve closed. Check all solder and compression joints on the tee and service valve for weeping. Open the service valve slowly. Check the wall plate connection for leaks. Open the outside tap and check the flow rate and pressure. Test the backflow preventer by checking it is present in the wall plate assembly; without it the installation fails the Water Fittings Regulations.

attrs.symptom: Joint weeps when pressure is restored
attrs.cause: The solder did not flow into the full circumference of the fitting, or a compression olive was not fully seated.
attrs.fix: Isolate and drain again. On a solder joint, clean, re-flux, and re-heat until solder flows visibly around the full joint. On a compression joint, tighten by a further quarter-turn.

attrs.symptom: Poor flow at the outside tap
attrs.cause: The service valve is only partially open, or the backflow preventer is partially blocked.
attrs.fix: Open the service valve fully (quarter-turn so the slot is in line with the pipe). If flow is still poor, remove the backflow preventer from the wall plate and inspect for debris.
